ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for braised pig trotters; closest parent 'pork food product' (FOODON_03302051) used as anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (pig meat and offal, cooked) is the closest defensible parent; no specific braised trotter code exists in the FoodEx2 catalogue.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for braised pig trotters as a prepared food item.

allergens: gluten (from soy sauce and Shaoxing wine)

serving.natural_language_portion: One typical restaurant serving of traditional braised pig trotters is approximately 250 g, comprising deboned, shaped braised trotter meat with skin and gelatinous connective tissue, served with braising sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Pork feet, simmered — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 10152)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for pork feet, simmered (NDB 10152 / FDC ID 168378) used as the primary nutrient anchor. Values adapted to reflect the braised preparation with soy sauce and sugar, which increases sodium and carbohydrate slightly relative to plain simmered trotters. Sodium adjusted upward to approximately 600 mg per 100 g to account for soy sauce braising liquid absorption. Carbohydrate adjusted to approximately 3.5 g per 100 g to account for rock sugar and dark soy sauce. Energy recalculated accordingly. All other macros and micronutrients retained from the USDA simmered pork feet baseline.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of protein- and fat-dominant foods — analogous estimate based on high-protein, high-fat braised pork
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for braised pig trotters. Estimated GI of 30 based on the University of Sydney GI Database principle that high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes have low GI values (typically 0–35). The carbohydrate content is approximately 3.5 g per 100 g, predominantly from soy sauce and rock sugar. Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (30 × 8.75 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 2.6, classified as low GL.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce braising
hypertension.guidance: Each 250 g serving provides approximately 1500 mg sodium, representing 65% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced-sauce servings.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 3.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g and an estimated GI of 30, braised pig trotters have a low glycemic load and are unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose excursions; however, the high fat content warrants attention for overall caloric management in individuals with Type 2 Diabetes.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: A 250 g serving contributes approximately 12.8 g saturated fat and 275 mg cholesterol alongside 1500 mg sodium, all of which are risk factors for cardiovascular disease; individuals with established CVD should consume this dish only occasionally and in small portions.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Traditional Braised Pig Trotters (老汤酱猪手, Lǎo tāng jiàng zhū shǒu) is a classic Chinese slow-cooked pork dish prepared by simmering whole pig trotters in an aged master broth (老汤) seasoned with soy sauce, dark soy sauce, rock sugar, Shaoxing rice wine, star anise, cinnamon, bay leaves, ginger, garlic, and five-spice powder. The extended braising process — often several hours — renders the collagen-rich skin and connective tissue into a soft, gelatinous texture while the meat becomes deeply tender and infused with the complex savoury-sweet flavour of the broth. After cooking, the trotters are deboned and shaped, making the dish easier to eat. The skin and gelatinous portions are rich in collagen and gelatin, while the lean meat contributes significant protein. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 222 kcal, 19.5 g protein, 14.8 g fat (of which 5.1 g is saturated), and 3.5 g carbohydrate. Sodium content is elevated at approximately 600 mg per 100 g due to soy sauce absorption during braising. The dish has a low glycemic index (estimated 30) owing to its high protein and fat content and minimal carbohydrate. Clinically, the high sodium and saturated fat content make it a concern for individuals with hypertension and cardiovascular disease, while the low carbohydrate and low GI profile make it relatively suitable for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes in moderate portions. This dish is popular in Chinese cuisine across mainland China, Hong Kong, Taiwan, and the Chinese diaspora communities of Singapore and Malaysia, where it is commonly served as a main course or as part of a shared family meal.
common_user_queries: How many calories in braised pig trotters?
Can diabetics eat braised pig trotters?
Is pig trotter high in sodium?
Is pig trotter high in cholesterol?
What is the GI of braised pork?

confidence_reason: No direct HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this specific braised pig trotter preparation. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy pork feet, simmered (FDC ID 168378), with sodium and carbohydrate adjusted upward to reflect soy sauce and rock sugar absorption during braising. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database principles for high-protein, high-fat, low-carbohydrate meat dishes. Confidence is medium rather than low because the USDA simmered pork feet baseline is a strong compositional anchor and the adjustments are well-reasoned.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA SR Legacy FDC ID 168378 (pork feet, simmered). Sodium adjusted from ~83 mg to ~600 mg per 100 g to reflect soy sauce braising; carbohydrate adjusted from ~0 g to ~3.5 g to reflect rock sugar and dark soy sauce. Energy recalculated using Atwater factors. 2. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 3. folate_mcg_dfe is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this food item. 7. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as pig trotters contain no plant-based fibre; this is naturally zero for a pure meat and skin product. 8. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — naturally zero as pork trotter meat and skin contain negligible vitamin C. 9. GI estimated at 30 based on University of Sydney GI Database analogous-food principles for high-protein, high-fat braised pork; no direct GI measurement found for this dish. 10. This dish is not halal as it contains pork. 11. The dish served at See Ya Tonight (今晚有局) Singapore is deboned and shaped, which is a restaurant-specific preparation variant.
